title: AI Pair Programming: My Journey with Aider
author: Jonathan Morales
text:
AI Pair Programming: My Journey with Aider
aider — AI Pair Programming in your terminal
I wrote 1 line of code and Aider wrote the other thousand…
I found myself searching for a better word to describe the relationship between a developer and their AI assistant. Terms like “assistant” or “copilot” feel insufficient when your AI partner is doing the heavy lifting while you focus on the vision and direction. After my recent experience with Aider, I discovered a new kind of programming partnership…
What is Aider?
Aider is a terminal-based AI pair programming tool that integrates directly with your codebase through Git. Unlike traditional AI coding assistants, Aider maintains context throughout your session, handles version control automatically, and can work across multiple files simultaneously.
Key Features:
- Integration with virtually every LLM including Claude, ChatGPT, DeepSeek, Llama and any model hosted by Ollama
- Seamless Git integration with automatic commits
- Context-aware modifications across multiple files
- Built-in version control with easy undo functionality
- Clean command interface with intuitive operations
The Experience: A Real-World Test Drive
I decided to put Aider to the test with my hobby game development website. My goal was simple: update the styling and add a review page for products. What followed was an interesting experiment in how AI could handle real-world development tasks.
Creating a Review Page from Scratch
My first request was straightforward — create a review page. Aider’s initial output surprised me by delivering a professionally structured page that went beyond basic requirements. It included thoughtful elements I hadn’t specified:
- “Key Topics Covered” section
- “Pros and Cons” breakdown
- “Who Should Read These” guidance
Like any first draft, it needed refinement. But that’s where Aider’s strength became apparent: its ability to maintain context through multiple revisions. We went back and forth, tweaking layouts and adjusting content, much like you would with a human pair programmer. The difference was that each iteration took seconds rather than minutes, and Aider never lost track of our previous changes or the project’s overall structure.
What impressed me wasn’t just the speed, but how the tool leveraged its understanding of my existing assets and codebase. Each suggestion aligned well with the existing codebase. While this capability stems from the underlying AI models’ ability to retain context and analyze attached files, Aider’s implementation made the whole process feel natural and seamless.
When I noticed it needed a detailed review section, I provided some context about my experience with “Game Coding Complete.” Aider seamlessly incorporated my feedback while maintaining the established style and structure.
Tackling Layout Challenges
The real test came when refactoring the landing page. The existing design had issues with whitespace, scaling, and responsiveness. Aider approached this systematically, though we did hit a small bump — a layout file reference error. This highlighted one of Aider’s strongest features: its Git integration.
The Power of Integrated Version Control
Every modification Aider makes, no matter how small, is committed with an AI-generated description. When we encountered the layout error, rolling back was as simple as typing /undo
. No manual Git commands, no hunting through commit history. After adding the correct layout file with /add
, we were back on track.
This version control integration transforms the development workflow. Need a fresh start? The /clear
command resets the session while maintaining file links. Want to push your changes? A simple git push
does the trick.
The Numbers: Productivity and Cost
For just 8 bucks a day you too can help a git project in your backlog starving for attention! 😢
Looking back at my session stats:
- Duration: Approximately 1 hour
- Commits Generated: 15
- Cost per PR: ~$0.06
- Total Session Cost: $0.90
The most surprising statistic? I wrote exactly one line of code — and it wasn’t even code, just an HTML image tag. Aider handled everything else.
A New Kind of Development Experience
This isn’t just about productivity metrics. Working with Aider feels fundamentally different from traditional development or even other AI coding tools. The experience is, surprisingly, peaceful and pleasant — words I haven’t associated with development in years.
The typical AI coding workflow often involves copying and pasting blocks of code, starting new sessions when context is lost, and juggling multiple chat windows. With Aider, I maintained context throughout, working with 2–4 files at a time without any cognitive overhead.
The Bottom Line
Looking back at my evening with Aider, what stands out isn’t just the metrics ($8/day, 11 PRs, one line of actual code written by me), but how it changed my relationship with coding. The experience reminded me of pair programming sessions from my early career — but without the scheduling conflicts and with the freedom to explore ideas at my own pace.
Is it perfect? No tool is. But for my specific use case — updating a hobby project that needed attention — it proved to be exactly what I needed. It’s left me curious about how tools like this might reshape not just how we write code, but how we think about the development process itself.
